items.myth: Maximum likelihood and maximum log-likelihood give different answers.
items.reality: Because log is strictly increasing, argmax_θ L(θ) = argmax_θ ℓ(θ) for any θ where L(θ) > 0; log is a monotonic reparameterization of the same objective.
items.example: Bernoulli data with 3/5 heads: p̂ = 0.6 maximizes both L(p) = p³(1−p)² and ℓ(p) = 3 log p + 2 log(1−p).

items.reality: Many classical models have closed-form MLE from setting ∇ℓ(θ) = 0; gradient descent is for cases without analytic solutions (deep nets, rich GLMs).
items.example: Gaussian mean MLE is the sample mean x̄—no iterative optimizer needed.

items.reality: Average cross-entropy over true labels is negative log-likelihood under a Categorical model; minimizing it is numerical MLE of softmax probabilities.
items.example: Three-class logits with true label 2: loss = −log softmax(z)₂—exactly −log P(y=2 | z).

items.myth: MLE is the same as least squares for every regression problem.
items.reality: OLS equals MLE for the conditional mean only under Gaussian noise with homoscedastic variance; other noise models imply different NLL losses.
items.example: Count data with Poisson noise should use Poisson log-likelihood, not squared error.
